facts.text: When the first settlers arrived in 1789, Lakewood was a dense forest along off‑beat Indian trails, long before Freehold, Tennent and Toms River became Revolutionary hotspots.
In 1880, Lakewood shed its Bricksburg name and emerged as a fashionable winter resort, drawing visitors from across the region.
In 1943, Aharon Kotler founded Beth Medrash Govoha, establishing Lakewood as a major center of Orthodox Judaism.
A devastating forest fire on April 20, 1940, destroyed more than 50 structures and scorched most of Lakewood’s southern half.
In 1924, the Sisters of Mercy moved their women's college from North Plainfield to Lakewood’s former George Jay Gould I estate, reshaping the town’s educational landscape.
